coredata.dc:title: Tailoring vinegar residue-derived all-carbon electrodes for efficient electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction to formate through heteroatom doping and defect enrichment

coredata.dc:description: Electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction (ECO2R) to formate is the most technically and economically feasible approach to achieve electrochemical CO2 value addition. Here, a few-layer graphene is prepared from vinegar residue. Then a series of heteroatom-doped vertical graphene electrodes (X-rGO, X=P/S/N/B/, NS/NP/NB, NSP/NSB/NPB/NSPB) are prepared. The NS-rGO has improved ECO2R to formate selectivity (Faraday Efficiency (FEHCOO −) = 78.7 %) thanks to the synergistic effect between N and S. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced into the electrode, the doped heteroatoms are further removed by high-temperature to form the defects-rich electrode (NS-CQDs-rGO-1100), which has better catalytic performance (FEHCOO −=90 %, stability over 10 h) with electrochemical double layer capacitance of 12.5 mF cm−2. The intrinsic effect of heteroatom doping and defects on the ECO2R activity of the electrodes are explored by density functional theory calculation. This work broadens the field of preparation of graphene and opens the door to the development of cost-effective electrocatalysts for efficient ECO2R.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction (ECO2R) to formate is the most technically and economically feasible approach to achieve electrochemical CO2 value addition. Here, a few-layer graphene is prepared from vinegar residue. Then a series of heteroatom-doped vertical graphene electrodes (X-rGO, X=P/S/N/B/, NS/NP/NB, NSP/NSB/NPB/NSPB) are prepared. The NS-rGO has improved ECO2R to formate selectivity (Faraday Efficiency (FEHCOO −) = 78.7 %) thanks to the synergistic effect between N and S. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced into the electrode, the doped heteroatoms are further removed by high-temperature to form the defects-rich electrode (NS-CQDs-rGO-1100), which has better catalytic performance (FEHCOO −=90 %, stability over 10 h) with electrochemical double layer capacitance of 12.5 mF cm−2. The intrinsic effect of heteroatom doping and defects on the ECO2R activity of the electrodes are explored by density functional theory calculation. This work broadens the field of preparation of graphene and opens the door to the development of cost-effective electrocatalysts for efficient ECO2R.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction (ECO2R) in combination with renewable energy is the best strategy to achieve net-zero carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions.[1–3] ECO2R is a complex reaction involving the transfer of multi-electrons that produces various reduction products. Among the various products, formate is currently the most economically viable product, as it is one of the basic organic chemical materials widely used in pesticides, leather, dyes, pharmaceuticals and other industries, as well as an important energy carrier for H2 or direct formic acid fuel cells.[4,5] The development and utilization of high-performance and low-cost electrocatalysts is crucial for ECO2R. So far, metal catalysts (Bi, Pb, Cd, In, Sn, etc.) have been reported to have high catalytic activity and selectivity for ECO2R to formate, but their high cost and immature durability limit their large-scale application.[6–8] Carbon materials (graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, etc.) are attractive electrocatalyst materials due to their low cost, low environmental toxicity, and wide pH range availability. [9,10].

Graphene has been widely used as a catalyst material for ECO2R thanks to its excellent electrical and mechanical properties.[11–15] For example, defective graphene (DG) has excellent ECO2R performance owing to its abundant catalytically active sites and strong CO2 adsorption capacity.[16] Because of the high cost, unsustainable, complicated process and demanding conditions of the current method of preparing graphene from natural graphite, a large number of studies on the preparation of graphene from waste biomass (including corn stover, oil palm biomass, and fruit peels, etc) as a carbon source has emerged. [12,17–19] For example, some researchers use sawdust (from birch) as a carbon source to prepare graphene with physical and chemical properties similar to industrial graphene.[20] Vinegar residue is the main by-product of vinegar production, has a huge yield and a pH value of 5–6, and traditional treatment methods (incineration, landfill) will affect the atmosphere and soil, endangering the safety of human life, so it is indispensable to treat vinegar dregs safely and effectively. [21,22] The use of vinasse-based cellulose (30–36 %) to prepare graphene can not only effectively deal with the waste pollutant vinasse, but also reduce the cost of graphene preparation and obtain a high graphene yield. There are usually two ways to improve the catalytic activity of graphene: expanding the number of exposed active sites and increasing the intrinsic activity.[23] In our previous work, three-dimensional (3D) vertical graphene with sufficient mechanical strength has been applied as a self-supporting electrode for efficient water splitting.[24–26] Moreover, the introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into 3D vertical graphene is more conducive to creating a larger specific surface area and exposing more active sites.[26] Therefore, it is advantageous to apply 3D vertical graphene loaded CQDs prepared from vinegar residue to ECO2R. Doping heteroatoms (N, B, P, S, etc.) in carbon materials can modify the electronic structure of the material and boost the intrinsic activity of the active sites.[27,28] Further, Co-doping of heteroatoms synergistically modulates the π-electrons in carbon atoms to even more enhance the electrocatalytic activity of the catalysts.[29,30] For instance, some researchers applied NP-co-doped carbon aerogels (NPCA) to ECO2R and found that NPCA has better electrochemical properties compared to N- or P-doped CA.[31] However, most of the current studies focus only on one type of single heteroatom doping, double heteroatom doping, etc., and there is no systematic study on the effect of different types of multi-heteroatom doping on electrode performance. Further, some researchers have used high temperature removal of heteroatoms (N, S, etc.) to create more topological defects in the electrodes for more efficient ECO2R applications.[32,33] For example, some researchers have used ECO2R to remove N heteroatoms from carbon materials at high temperatures to form defects, and studies have shown that carbon defects play a decisive role in the performance of electrodes ECO2R.[34] Different temperatures have important research significance on the defect formation effect and the performance of electrode ECO2R.

In this paper, graphene is received from cellulose extracted from vinegar residue. And the graphene is further prepared into a heteroatom-doping vertical graphene electrode (X-rGO, X=N/S/P/B, NS/NP/NB, NSP/NSB/NPB, NSPB) for ECO2R. N/NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes are prepared by introducing CQDs into the electrodes, and then the defective carbon electrode is prepared by pyrolysis at different high temperatures for ECO2R to formate. The selectivity of the NS-CQDs-rGO electrode for formate is found to be up to 90 % by electrochemical tests. The effect of heteroatom doping and topological defects on the ECO2R properties of the electrodes and their intrinsic causes are investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. This work delivers a novel approach for the preparation of high-performance biomass graphene, and perspectives on the design and development of multifunctional electrocatalysts for the production of value-added chemicals by ECO2R.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of X-rGO (X=N/S/P/B, NS/NP/NB, NSP/NSB/NPB, NSPB)
ce:section.ce:para: Vinegar residue was obtained from South Xiaozhang Village Guo Junqi Sauce and Vinegar Factory. The reagents used in the experiment and their basic information were shown in Table S1. Vinegar residue-based cellulose was extracted from vinegar residue by the acid-base method, and graphene was prepared from cellulose by a three-step calcination method (More details in the supporting information). Graphene oxide (GO) is obtained by oxidation of the prepared graphene by the modified Hummers method.[35] X-rGO (X=N/S/B/P) was obtained by directional freezing and high-temperature calcination. First, five centrifuge tubes were filled with five portions of 0.12 g of GO and 0.12 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), followed by 10 mL of DI water. The centrifuge tube was ultrasonically dispersed for 2 h to form a homogeneous GO-PVA dispersion with a density of 12:12 mg mL−1. During ultrasonic dispersion, 0.03 g CH4N2O or 0.07 g H3BO3 or 2 mL dimethyl sulfoxide (500 times dilution) or 2 mL phytic acid (500 times dilution) was added to four of the centrifuge tubes, respectively, and no reagents were added to the other centrifuge tube for comparison. Then 300 μL anhydrous ethanol was dripped into each centrifuge tube. After dispersion, the mixture was transferred to the mould prepared in advance, and the mould was frozen in an environment filled with liquid nitrogen. Then the mold was put into a vacuum freeze dryer to freeze dry for more than 48 h to get graphene aerogel. Finally, graphene aerogel was placed into a vacuum tube high-temperature sintering furnace, and the tube furnace was heated to 150 °C for 30 min under Ar atmosphere, then the temperature was raised to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min for 3 h of high temperature annealing. When the tube furnace dropped to room temperature, X-rGO (X=S/P/N/B) can be obtained.

X-rGO (X=NS/NP/NB, NSP/NSB/NPB, NSPB) was prepared using the same directed freezing and heat treatment methods. By controlling the mass of N atoms in each dopant, during ultrasonic dispersion, 0.012 g NH4H2PO4, 0.045 g NH4B5O8·4H2O, and 0.121 g C3H7NO2S were added to obtain NP-rGO, NB-rGO, NS-rGO, separately. The mass taken by the diatomic dopant is halved or taken by one-third to ensure that the mass of N atoms doped by multi-heteroatom doping and dual-heteroatom doping are the same. During ultrasonic dispersion, 0.006 g NH4H2PO4 and 0.0225 g NH4B5O8·4H2O were added to obtain NPB-rGO, 0.0605 g C3H7NO2S and 0.006 g NH4H2PO4 were added to obtain NSP-rGO, 0.0605 g C3H7NO2S, and 0.0225 g NH4B5O8·4H2O were added to obtain NSB-rGO, 0.004 g NH4H2PO4, 0.015 g NH4B5O8·4H2O, and 0.0403 g C3H7NO2S were added to obtain NSPB-rGO.

0.12 g GO and 0.12 g PVA were added to the centrifuge tubes, followed by the addition of 5 mL of prepared CQDs (Supporting Information) and 5 mL of DI water to the centrifuge tubes, which were then dispersed by ultrasonication for 2 h to form a homogeneous dispersion. After the GO was dispersed completely, 30 mg of urea or 121 mg of l-cysteine were added into centrifugal tubes and sonicated again for a while. After the solution was homogeneous, 300uL of absolute ethanol was added. Then the sol was added to the mold, the mold was frozen with liquid nitrogen, and the mold was put into a vacuum freeze dryer for freeze-drying for about 48 h to obtain CQDs-GO aerogel. Finally, the aerogel is placed in a vacuum tube sintering furnace and argon gas is passed through for 30 min to remove the air in the tube. The temperature was raised to 150 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min for 30 min, and then the temperature was raised to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min for 3 h. When the furnace temperature drops to room temperature, the samples are removed to give N-CQDs-rGO or NS-CQDs-rGO.

The prepared N-CQDs-rGO and NS-CQDs-rGO were placed in a vacuum tubular high-temperature sintering furnace and heated up to 800, 900, 1000, and 1100 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min, and then the samples were taken out after the temperature of the tubular furnace was lowered to room temperature to obtain N/NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU8010) was applied to observe and analyze the surface morphology of electrodes, and SEM Mapping was used to confirm the composition of electrode surface elements. X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD, D8 DISCOVER, Cu Kα radiation) was used to analyze the phase and crystal of the electrode. The molecular structure of the electrodes was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi) and Raman spectrum.

Electrochemical tests of the electrode were performed by the Shanghai Chenhua Electrochemical Workstation (CHI 660E). The electrochemical test was executed in a closed two-chamber (H-type) electrolytic cell, with a cathode working electrode and a reference electrode chamber, an anode opposing electrode chamber, and a proton exchange membrane separation (Nafion 117) between the two chambers. All electrochemical tests were preceded by a gas-tightness check of the reaction system. The prepared catalyst was a self-supporting electrode directly used as a working electrode for electrochemical testing, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) was used as a reference electrode and corrected before testing, and a platinum sheet electrode (1 × 1 cm2) was used as a counter electrode. All electrochemical CO2 reduction tests were performed in a CO2-saturated 0.1 M KHCO3 (pH=8.7) electrolyte. The measured potential was converted to the potential relative to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) by the following formula:
(1)
E
RHE
=
E
Ag / A g C l
+ 0.197 + 0.0591 × p H

Before the test, N2 or CO2 was passed into the cathode chamber of the H-type electrolyzer for half an hour to exhaust the air from the electrolyzer and linear voltammetric scans (LSV) were carried out under N2-saturated electrolyte and CO2-saturated electrolyte, respectively. A cyclic voltammetric scan (CV) was performed under CO2-saturated electrolyte.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was evaluated by electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl), which was calculated from CV test data at different sweep speeds (5–50 mVs−1). LSV was used to test the ECO2R catalytic performance of the prepared electrodes at a scanning speed of 5 mV s−1. The Tafel slope was determined by fitting the Tafel plot obtained by redrawing the LSV curve. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode as well as the solution resistance (Rs) of the electrolyte were represented by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The stability of the electrodes was tested by Chronoamperometry.

The liquid phase product of carbon dioxide reduction of the electrode was analyzed by Ion Chromatograph System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Dionex Aquion). The Faraday effect of formic acid at a given potential is calculated by the following equation [36]:
(2)
FE
HCOO
-
= n × N ×
F Q
× 100 %
where n is the amount of HCOO– [mol], it is equal to the concentration of formate in the electrolyte (CHCOO –) and the volume of the electrolyte (30 mL). N is the amount of electrons transferred by the product, and N is equal to 2 for HCOOH. F denotes Faraday’s constant (96485C mol−1). Q is the amount of charge obtained by integrating the current–time curve over Faraday’s sampling time [C].

The gas-phase products were detected by gas chromatography (GC, Changzhou Pano Instrument Co., A60) using a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and flame ionization detector (FID) after the 2 h of electrolysis reaction. The faradaic efficiencies of H2 and CO are obtained by the following equation[37]:
FE
CO /
H 2
=
NFVv
P 0
QRT
× 100 %
where V is the volume of the gas compartment. ν is the volume concentration of H2 or CO in the gas. F is the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1). N is the amount of electrons transferred by the product, and N is equal to 2 for H2/CO. P0 is the atmospheric pressure (101 × 103 Pa). T is the room temperature (298.15 K). R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1). And Q is the charge passed through the catalyst.

All the Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP).[38] The database of Materials Studio software was used to construct the basic model of graphene monoliths. The lattice constant in the X-axis and Y-axis directions in all the calculation models in this paper was 20 Å angstroms, and a vacuum layer of 15 Å was added in the Z-axis direction. The projector augmented wave method in conjugation with a generalized gradient approximation of exchange–correlation functional in the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) form was adopted. The one-electron wave functions were expanded using a plane-wave basis set with an energy cut-off of 500 eV. The convergence units of energy and force during structural relaxation were set to 10−5 and 10−4. 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack sampled k-points were available for all of the slabs system.

The reaction free energy for each species was computed from the standard formula:
(3)
G =
E
DFT
+ Z P E - T S
where EDFT is the total electronic energy, T stands for temperature, S is the entropy, and ZPE denotes the zero-point energy. ZPE is calculated with the vibrational frequency of substrate, adsorbates and molecules within DFT.

Fig. 1 (a) (b) shows the SEM image of graphene prepared based on vinegar residue-based cellulose, the prepared graphene has a relatively smooth surface, fewer lamellae and thinner thickness with some folds. It shows the successful preparation of fewer layers of graphene with smooth surfaces. The peak at 2θ = 26.381° in the XRD spectrum of graphene (Fig. 1(c)) belongs to the (002) crystalline of the graphite-C crystal phase of graphene (JCPDS card number 41–1487), which suggests that the vinegar residue cellulose undergoes graphitization to produce graphene during high-temperature calcination. The sharper peaks on the XRD pattern show the crystalline structure of graphene, indicating that graphene has a higher crystallinity and a more ordered lattice structure. A characteristic π to π* transition of the aromatic CC bond of the graphene structure causes the appearance of a UV–visible absorption peak at ∼269 nm (Fig. 1(d)).[39] The molecular structure and properties of the prepared graphene were characterized by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1(e)). The strength ratio of IG/ID is proportional to the crystallinity of the carbon material.[17] Three characteristic peaks can be observed in the graphene sample, which is the D band at ∼1340 cm−1, the G band at ∼1580 cm−1 and the 2D band at ∼2679 cm−1. The Raman spectrum shows that the intensity of the G peak is stronger than that of the D peak, suggesting that the prepared graphene has a regular graphitic property. The IG/ID value of 3.05 suggests a high graphitization degree of the product. The high degree of graphitization of the few layers of graphene indicates that the prepared graphene has high mechanical strength and good electrical conductivity.[40] The relatively symmetrical profile of the 2D peak (second-order peaks in Raman spectroscopy) and the I2D/IG intensity ratio (∼0.37) suggest that only a very small amount of stacking of graphene flakes occurs.[20] All measurements show the successful preparation of few-layer graphene with a high degree of graphitization from cellulose extracted from vinegar residue.

The SEM-mapping diagram (Fig. 2 (a)(b)(c)(d)) of the electrodes shows that heteroatom (N, S, P, B) is successfully doped into rGO, indicating the satisfactory preparation of X-rGO (X=N, S, P, B) electrode. Table S2 (Supporting information) shows the content of each element in the doped electrode. Fig. 2(e)(f) shows that the prepared electrodes have an obvious layered three-dimensional vertical structure and smooth surface with folds. The layered vertical structure of the electrode provides a channel for electrolyte infiltration, CO2 adsorption and product release, accelerating the ECO2R reaction.[41] The three-dimensional vertical graphene prepared by graphene with a high degree of graphitization has high conductivity and high mechanical strength, and can be employed as a self-supporting electrode for ECO2R reaction.[40] The XRD plots (Fig. 3 (a)) of the electrode X-rGO (X=N, S, P, B) all have the peak located at 2θ = 26.381° corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphite-C crystalline phase of graphene (JCPDS#41-1487), which is in agreement with the XRD plots of the X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB, NSP, NSB, NSPB) electrodes in Figure S1, suggesting that the doping of heteroatoms does not change the crystalline phase structure of the carbon material.[42] More, the N-rGO and NS-rGO electrodes are further added with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and then further calcined at high temperatures to form the defect-rich electrodes N-CQD-rGO-X and NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100), respectively, and the crystalline phase structures of the catalysts are basically kept unchanged (Figure S2 and Fig. 3 (c)). The Raman spectra (Fig. 3(b)) of X-rGO (X=N, P, S, B) electrodes are similar and consist of two main peaks located at ∼1327 cm−1 (D peak) and ∼1586 cm−1 (G peak). The presence of the D peak indicates the defects such as edges and voids in the prepared electrodes.[43] Generally, the magnitude of the ID/IG value is taken to represent the degree of defects in the electrode, and a higher ID/IG value indicates more defects in the electrode.[44] The ID/IG values of X-rGO are presented in the following order: N-rGO (1.27) > S-rGO (1.22) > P-rGO (1.20) > B-rGO (1.17) > rGO (0.96), indicating that heteroatom doping in graphene creates more defects, and N doping is more conducive to increasing the defect site of the electrode and is more conducive to ECO2R reaction. N has a small atomic radius that can be easily added to different locations of the carbon matrix, creating strong electron interactions with the carbon atoms,[27] since the electronegativity of N atoms is higher than that of C atoms, N atoms have a high charge density and high polarity, so the induction of N atoms into carbon materials facilitates the transfer of charge to the CO2 molecules.[45] Based on the above, N-doped vertical graphene may exhibit better ECO2R catalytic performance. High-temperature calcination of NS-CQDs-rGO formed defect-rich electrodes NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100), whose Raman spectra (Fig. 3(d)) shows that the ID/IG of the electrodes increases as the temperature of calcination increases, implying that the defects formed on the surface of the electrodes gradually increase with the increase of temperature.[46] Among them, the NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 electrode has the largest ID/IG value (1.48), indicating a higher number of surface defects, suggesting that it may have better ECO2R performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS spectra analysis of NS-CQDs-rGO and NS-CQDs-rGO-1100
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS full spectrum of the NS-CQDs-rGO electrode in Fig. 4 (a) shows the presence of C, O, N, and S elements, indicating that N S is successfully doped into the CQDs-rGO electrode. The XPS of the NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 electrode shows that the NS elements in the electrode are greatly reduced after the high-temperature calcination of the NS-CQDs-rGO at 1100℃, which indicates that NS heteroatoms are removed to form topological defects during the high-temperature calcination process. The N1s profile of the electrode in Fig. 4(b) can be fitted to three forms of N, namely pyridinic N (398.0 eV), pyrrolic N (399.1 eV), and graphitic N (400.8 eV). The high-temperature-treated NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 electrode shows a decrease in the content of all forms of N compared to the NS-CQDs-rGO electrode, and the total N content is reduced from 4.43 % to 0.78 % (Fig. 4(d)), indicating that the high temperature effectively takes out the doped ground N atoms for the formation of topological defects. The S2p maps (Fig. 4(c)) at 164.1 eV, 165.3 eV, and 167.6 eV are attributed to the CSC bonding of S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 and C-SOx-C, respectively. Combined with Fig. 4(d), all forms of S doping are reduced with the total S content decreasing from 2.08 % to 0.33 %, indicating that the doped S atoms are also essentially removed during high temperature calcination to form the defects. XPS and Raman data indicate that the removal of heteroatoms (N, S, etc.) by heating is an effective method for preparing carbon materials containing inherent carbon defects. The removal of the heteroatoms leads to the reconstruction of the remaining carbon atoms, a process that results in pentagonal, heptagonal and octagonal carbon atom arrangements and substantial carbon disorder.

The effect of different heteroatom doping in single heteroatom doped electrodes on the performance of catalyst ECO2R is first explored by electrochemical tests. The LSV plots (Supporting information, Figure S3(a)) show that all electrodes produce substantially greater current densities when electrolyzed in CO2-saturated electrolyte than when electrolyzed in N2-saturated electrolyte. It indicates that the ECO2R process occurs at the electrode surface. Moreover, N-rGO has the maximum current density and the minimum initial potentials, indicating that N-rGO has enhanced ECO2R activity. Furthermore, N-rGO has the fastest reaction rate and activity towards ECO2R formate (Figure S3 (b)). The Cdl value (Figure S3(c)) of the N-rGO electrode is also above that of X-rGO (X=S, P, B), suggesting that the N-rGO has the maximum ECSA and high intrinsic catalytic activity. N-rGO has the smallest charge transfer resistance, indicating that N-rGO has the highest conductivity and fastest reaction rate (Figure S3(d)). The doping of the N atom better enlarges the electrochemically active surface area, and leads to a lower charge transfer rate of the electrode, which in turn boosts the ECO2R catalytic activity of the electrode. N-rGO is initially considered the basis for subsequent electrode preparation due to its excellent intrinsic activity and enhanced ECO2R catalytic activity. Chronoamperometry tests are performed in CO2-saturated 0.1 M KHCO3 solution at different potentials to further evaluate the ECO2R activity and selectivity of the X-rGO(X=N, S, P, B) electrode. 1H NMR results (Figure S4) show that formate is the only liquid product of the electrode ECO2R process.[47] Figure S5(a)-(c) shows that the N-rGO electrode has the maximum formate generation Faraday efficiency FEHCOO − (60 %) at −0.75 V vs. RHE, and the partial current density (jHCOO −) of the electrode ECO2R for formate production is −8.3 mA cm−2 at this potential, which is superior to that of X-rGO (X=S, P, B). The stability of X-rGO (X=N, P, S, B) and rGO for ECO2R is evaluated by continuous electrolysis at −0.75, −0.75, −0.8, −0.8, and −0.9 V vs.RHE for 10 h (Figure S5(d)). All the prepared electrodes showed good stability over 10 h, which demonstrated the scientific validity of the materials themselves. All electrochemical tests showed that N-rGO has better electrochemical performance and higher FEHCOO −. The excellent electrochemical activity and FE of N-rGO make it a basis for the next experiments.

The LSV plot (Fig. 5 (a)) shows that the current density of X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB) electrodes in CO2-saturated electrolyte is substantially increased compared to N2-saturated electrolyte thanks to ECO2R. The magnitude of their current densities in CO2-saturated 0.1 M KHCO3 at the same potential shows the following order: NS-rGO>NP-rGO>NB-rGO. The larger current density of NS-rGO suggests that the synergistic effect of N and S in graphene facilitates the activation of CO2 molecules, which in turn favors NS-rGO for the ECO2R process with superior selectivity.[45] NS-rGO, and NP-rGO have better current densities than N-rGO, indicating that co-doping of N, S and N, P is beneficial to enhance the catalytic activity of electrodes for ECO2R,[31,48] and that the enhancement effect of N, S co-doping on the performance of electrodes is more obvious. NB-rGO has a similar current density to that of N-rGO, indicating that the enhancement of ECO2R catalytic activity of the electrodes by N, B co-doping is not significantly effective or even slightly inhibited. The better ECO2R catalytic activity of NS-rGO compared to single heteroatom doping (N- or S-doped) may be explained by the fact that N and S co-doping increases the maximum spin density on the carbon, resulting in more catalytically active sites.[29] Tafel slopes are used to explore ECO2R kinetics at different electrodes. It is widely accepted that lower tafel slopes usually imply faster kinetics of formate generation.[49] Fig. 5(b) shows the Tafel slope of X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB), which exhibits the same order of consistent as LSV, where NS-rGO displays a much smaller Tafel slope (119 mV dec−1) than that of NP-rGO (138 mV dec−1), and NB-rGO (172 mV dec−1), confirming the best activity of NS-rGO toward ECO2R to formate due to the introduction of more reactive sites by N, S co-doping. Moreover, the tafel slope of the NS-rGO electrode is very near the theoretical value (118 mV dec−1), believes that the rate-determining step for the progression of ECO2R on the NS-rGO electrode is single-electron transfer. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the electrodes is evaluated by electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl).[45]In Fig. 5(c), the Cdl value of the X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB) electrode is 5.61 mF cm−2, 5.00 mF cm−2, and 3.70 mF cm−2, respectively. NS-rGO has the greatest Cdl, suggesting that the NS-rGO has the maximum ECSA and high intrinsic catalytic activity. It is beneficial in exposing the catalytically active sites to the electrode surface during the ECO2R process. The EIS spectrum (Fig. 5(d)) shows that NS-rGO has the smallest charge transfer resistance, indicating that NS-rGO has the highest conductivity and fastest reaction rate. These results indicate that NS-rGO is more favorable for the formation of formate at relatively low overpotentials compared to other electrodes.[50] All electrochemical tests show that NS-rGO has a high specific surface area and low interfacial charge transfer resistance, which is more suitable for the occurrence of the ECO2R process on the electrode surface. Compared with single-heteroatom-doped electrodes, di-heteroatom doping is more advantageous for improving improving the ECO2R catalytic property of electrodes.

Chronogalvanic tests are performed on X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB) at different potentials in 0.1 M KHCO3 solution saturated with CO2. Fig. 5(e) shows the concentration mean change curve of formate produced in the electrolytic cell after half an hour at various application potentials. As the potential increases, the FEHCOO − changes (Fig. 5(f)) of X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB) like a volcanic curve. The maximum FEHCOO − of NS-rGO can reach 78.7 % with a partial current density (jHCOO −) of −7.5 mA cm−2 at −0.60 V vs.RHE (Fig. 5(g)), which is significantly above that of NP-rGO (67.17 %) and NB-rGO (57.65 %). Moreover, the FEHCOO − of the NS-rGO electrode is better than that of the recently reported electrode in Figure S5.[51–56] The FEHCOO − value remained above 68 % at an applied potential of −1.1 V vs. RHE with a jHCOO − of −25 mA cm−2, indicating a wide range of applicable potentials for ECO2R at the NS-rGO electrode. The stability of the NS-rGO, NP-rGO, and NB-rGO electrodes for the ECO2R process is evaluated by 10 h of continuous electrolysis in H-cells at −0.60, −0.65, and −0.7 V vs.RHE, respectively (Fig. 5(h)). During the continuous electrolysis period of 10 h, the current attenuation of NS-rGO can be negligible, and the average FEHCOO − is about 78.7 %. The catalytic effect of ECO2R of the multi-heteroatom-doped catalysts was lower than that of NS-rGO due to the competition between the heteroatoms. NS-rGO has better overall catalytic performance for ECO2R. Depending on the earlier results and analyses, the high ECO2R catalytic activity and excellent FEHCOO − of NS-rGO may be caused by three factors: (1) Doping S in N-doped graphene can boost the asymmetric spin density of graphene, because the polarizability of S atoms is greater than that of N and C atoms, thereby improving performance; (2) NS-rGO prepared by graphene with high graphitization has high conductivity and accelerates the ECO2R reaction; (3) synergistic interaction between N and S. All electrochemical test results show that di-heteroatom doping is more conducive to enhancing the ECO2R electrocatalytic activity of the electrode than single heteroatom doping. To further explore the effect of polyheteroatom doping on the performance of rGO electrodes, ECO2R tests are performed on X-rGO (X=NSP, NSB, NPB, NSPB) electrodes using the same test method (Figure S9-S10). Combined with the XPS results (Figure S7-S8), the amount of doped N atoms remains the same, but the final doped effective N atoms are reduced due to the competition between dopants and “neutralization” between different doping types results in better ECO2R performance for NSP-rGO than for NSB-rGO, and lower ECO2R catalytic performance for NSP-rGO and NSB-rGO than for NS-rGO. Among the heteroatom-doped electrodes, the NS-rGO electrode has better ECO2R properties.

Figure S11(a)-(d) shows the effect of electrodes on ECO2R after the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on top of N/NS-rGO. N-CQDs-rGO and NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes have a more sensitive ECO2R response compared to the N-rGO and NS-rGO as evidenced by, increased electrode currents, accelerated reaction kinetics, improved electrode Cdl of the electrode and lowering the resistance of the electrode. The introduction of CQDs into the electrode can increase the ECSA and electrical conductivity of the electrode as well as accelerate the reaction kinetics of the electrode, thereby enhancing the electrocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of the electrode. The LSV plots of the N-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrodes show that a CO2 reduction reaction occurs on the surface of the electrodes when they are tested in a CO2-saturated electrolyte so that their current densities are higher than those of the electrodes tested in an N2-saturated electrolyte at the same potentials (Figure S12(a)). Moreover, the current density of the electrodes at the same potential shows the following pattern: N-CQDs-rGO-1100 > N-CQDs-rGO-1000 > N-CQDs-rGO-900 > N-CQDs-rGO-800. The higher the temperature, the greater the degree of defects produced on the electrode surface, and the better the electrochemical performance of the electrode, which indicated that defects formed by high temperature denitration had a beneficial effect on the CO2 reduction has a favorable effect. The Tafel slopes (Figure S12(b)) of the electrodes show that the Tafel slopes of N-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800,900,1000,1100) are similar and all are close to the theoretical value of 118 mV dec−1, suggesting that the rate-determining step for CO2 reduction at all the electrodes is the first single-electron transfer process. The change in the CO2 reduction performance of N-CQDs-rGO treated at different temperatures may be attributed to changing the material properties of the electrodes rather than changing the pathway of CO2 reduction at the electrodes. The Cdl of N-CQDs-rGO-800 (7.10 mF cm−2), N-CQDs-rGO-900 (7.70 mF cm−2), N-CQDs-rGO-1000 (8.41 mF cm−2), and N-CQDs-rGO-1100 (9.49 mF cm−2) electrodes are all elevated compared to N-CQDs-rGO (6.16 mF cm−2), indicating that treating N-CQDs-rGO at high temperature is beneficial for the electrode to produce more catalytically active sites (Figure S12(c)). The Cdl of the electrodes is slightly elevated with the increase of the treatment temperature, in which the Cdl of N-CQDs-rGO-1100 is the largest, indicating that it has the largest ECSA, suggesting that the higher the temperature, the more catalytically active sites are generated on the electrode surface and the larger the electrochemically active surface area. The EIS plots (Figure S12(d)) of the electrodes show that the EIS values (9.52 Ω, 7.94 Ω, 6.70 Ω, and 4.74 Ω) of N-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) are smaller than those of N-CQDs-rGO (10.04 Ω). This result indicates that the high-temperature treatment of N-CQDs-rGO can improve the conductivity of the electrode to a certain extent, and the higher the treatment temperature, the lower the resistance of the electrode and the higher the charge transfer efficiency. The high temperature of N-CQDs-rGO can increase the catalytic active site of the electrode, and the higher the temperature, the greater the degree of defect formation, and the greater the impact on the conductivity of the electrode and the performance of electrocatalytic CO2 reduction. The mean value of formic acid concentration after three repetitions of the test produced by the electrodes after electrolysis at different applied potentials for 30 min increases gradually with the increase of the potential (Figure S13(a)). Compared with the N-CQDs-rGO electrode, the defect-rich N-CQDs-rGO electrode generated by further treatment (N-CQDs-rGO-X, X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) produces a greater concentration of formate at the same potential, and higher the treatment temperature, greater the concentration. The Faraday efficiency (FE, Figure S13(b)) of the electrode ECO2R for the generation of formate shows that the FEHCOO − of the N-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrode is enhanced from 65.27 % to 70.28 %, 72.20 %, 77.19 %, 81.81 % when the N-CQDs-rGO is treated at high temperature. The FEHCOO − of the electrode has a significant enhancement after high-temperature treatment, demonstrating the decisive role of defects in ECO2R. The partial current densities (Figure S13(c)) of the electrodes at the potential having the highest FEHCOO − are −6.62, −8.79, −9.62, −8.47, −9.04 mA cm−2. The FEHCOO − as well as the current densities of all electrodes in the electrolysis of CO2 to formic acid in CO2-saturated KHCO3 electrolyte can be kept stable for more than 10 h (Figure S13(d)). Treating the N-CQDs-rGO electrode at a high temperature produces defects that are more favorable to the reaction of formic acid from ECO2R, and the higher the temperature, the more defects are produced improved FEHCOO −. These results, along with the density functional theory (DFT) calculations, reveal the decisive role of defects rather than the previously invoked N species in ECO2R.

The LSV plots (Fig. 6 (a)) of NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrodes show that the NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes treated with high temperature have superior electrochemical activity, and the enhancement of the electrochemical activity of the electrodes is not obvious when the temperatures are not high enough (800, 900, and 1000 °C), and when the electrodes are treated with the temperature of 1100 °C, the electrochemical activity of the electrodes can be obtained with a more obvious enhancement. The higher the temperature, the more complete the removal of heteroatoms, the more defects formed on the electrode surface, and the more conducive to the CO2 reduction reaction of the electrode, indicating the decisive role of defects in the CO2 reduction process. The Tafel plot (Fig. 6(b)) shows that several electrodes have similar Tafel slopes, indicating that different temperature treatments do not change the CO2 reduction process of the electrodes, that is, the rate-determining step of the electrodes for CO2 reduction is still a single electron transfer process. The Cdl values of NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) are 9.20, 9.85, 10.91, and 12.50 mF cm−2, respectively (Fig. 6(c)). The Cdl value of NS-CQDs-rGO-800 is slightly higher than those of NS-CQDs-rGO (8.54 mF cm−2), but the difference was not significant. NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 has the largest Cdl value and the most obvious enhancement, indicating that the higher the temperature, the more defects are formed and the higher the number of active sites on the electrode surface. The impedance spectra (Fig. 6(d)) show that the charge transfer resistances of NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) are smaller than that of NS-CQDs-rGO. High-temperature treatment can enhance the conductivity of the electrode, and the enhancement effect is most obvious when the treatment temperature is 1100 °C, and the charge transfer resistance of the electrode is the smallest. In NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes, when the treatment temperatures are not high enough (800, 900, 1000 °C), the effects on the material properties of the electrodes and the performance of the electrode CO2 reduction are not significant, suggesting that the heteroatom removal in the electrodes is not complete at these temperatures, possibly since S atoms are more difficult to remove than N atoms. When the temperature is high enough (1100 °C), the heteroatoms in the electrode are removed to form defects, which provides more active sites for the electrode reaction and is more conducive to enhancing the CO2 reduction performance of the electrode.

The average values of formic acid concentrations produced by the NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrode after 30 min of ECO2R process in CO2-saturated 0.1 M KHCO3 solution after three repetitions of the test is shown in Fig. 6(e). The concentration of formate produced by the electrode increases as the applied potential increases. The FEHCOO − (Fig. 6(f)) of the NS-CQDs-rGO electrode reached the maximum (81.86 %) when the applied potential is −0.7 V vs. RHE, and at this time, the partial current density (Fig. 6(g)) of formate produced by its ECO2R is −9.66 mA cm−2. The NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrodes achieve the maximum FEHCOO − when the applied potentials are −0.75, −0.7, −0.7, −0.65 V vs. RHE, individually. Among them, the NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 electrode has a maximum FEHCOO − up to 90 % and lower FE for the generation of CO or H2, indicating that the electrode has a better selectivity for the generation of formate. Significantly, the FEHCOO − at NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrodes are all enhanced compared with those of NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes, indicating that during the formation of defects by high-temperature treatment of doped graphene, N and S play similar roles, and both of them can be removed at high temperatures for the formation of defects more conducive to the reaction. And higher the temperature, the more thoroughly the heteroatoms are removed and the more defects are formed, which is more favorable for the ECO2R formate reaction. The NS-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100) electrode has superior Faraday efficiency for ECO2R generation of formate compared to N-CQDs-rGO-X (X=800, 900, 1000, 1100). Introducing S into N-CQDs-rGO can increase the number of heteroatoms in graphene to a certain extent, and removing the heteroatoms by high-temperature heat treatment under the same conditions for NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes and N-CQDs-rGO electrodes, NS-CQDs-rGO electrodes can form more defects, which will lead to a more excellent ECO2R performance. All electrodes can maintain the relative stability of current density and Faraday efficiency for more than 10 h during the ECO2R formate generation process (Fig. 6 (h)). The NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 electrode has an optimal ECO2R performance and a large Faraday efficiency for formic acid generation, and this Faraday efficiency is higher than that reported so far for metallic[7,57–67](Fig. 7 (a)) and non-metallic[15,41,68–72] (Fig. 7(b)) electrodes. Clearly, the FEHCOO − of our prepared defect-rich electrode NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 is significantly higher than that of recently reported metal catalysts. In addition, comparison with all-carbon materials shows that ECO2R to formate in heteroatom-doped carbon materials is a feasible and scientifically sound option, and the FEHCOO − of our prepared electrodes also has a significant advantage over that of the reported all-carbon electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of NS-rGO before and after ECO2R reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The composition of NS-rGO before and after the ECO2R reaction is detected by XPS. The C1s peak (Figure S14(a)) at 284.6 eV matches the CC/CC bond, the peak at 286.6 eV matches the CN/CS bond, and the peak at 288.6 eV matches the CO/CO bond.[73,74] The intense CC/CC band and the slight peak of the CO/CO band demonstrate that the GO is reduced during the high-temperature sintering. The appearance of the CN/CS bond confirms the successful realization of the co-doping of N and S atoms in the vertical graphene structure. There is no significant change in CN/CS bonds before and after ECO2R, suggesting that the electrode remained steady during the ECO2R process over a long period of time. In Figure S14(b), the high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) spectrum of N 1 s is divided into three peaks of pyrrolic N (399.1 eV), pyridinic N (398.0 eV), and Graphitic N (400.8 eV).[31] Generally, pyridine N is recognized as a key factor for the excellent catalytic properties of N-doped carbon materials, as it can initiate defects and generate more active sites.[75] More, graphitic-N can strengthen the electrical conductivity of graphene, thus contributing to the catalytic process.[76] The content (Figure S14(d)) of N atoms detected in NS-rGO before the reaction is 4.03 %, and the content of N atoms after the reaction is 3.23 %, with the most obvious change of pyridinic N in N species before and after the reaction is attributed to the participation of C atoms near pyridine N in the reaction, suggesting that pyridine N is the main active site in N species. In Figure S14(c), the fitted peaks with lower binding energy in the S 2p spectra (S2p 2p3/2 at 164.1 eV and S2p 2p1/2 at 165.3 eV) can be attributed to CSC band, while the peak located at 167.6 eV corresponds to the oxidized species (C-SOx-C).[77] The successful doping of S atoms in graphene can induce the creation of more defect sites, resulting in electrodes with more electrochemically active sites. The content (Figure S14(d)) of S atoms detected in NS-rGO before the reaction is 1.74 %, and the content of S atoms after the reaction is 1.66 %, the content changes before and after the reaction are not significant, suggesting that the S species is not the main active site of NS-rGO for ECO2R. It can be postulated from XPS analysis that the co-doped heteroatoms have a synergistic effect that may boost the properties of the electrodes. Before and after the ECO2R reaction of the NS-rGO electrode, the content of N species changed more significantly than that of S species, indicating that N species (especially pyridinic N) are the main active sites of NS-rGO in the ECO2R process, and S species are a stimulator that enhances the ECO2R catalytic activity and selectivity of the N species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion of the effects of electrodes using DFT calculations
ce:section.ce:para: The change in the reaction free energy of ECO2R over the electrode is first calculated using density-functional theory (DFT) to examine the intrinsic reasons for N doping to boost the properties of ECO2R. The change in free energy of reaction for ECO2R generation of formate from these active sites is then calculated. It is worth mentioning that the free energy transition values of the interstitials forming intermediates reflect the key to the limit potentials (e.g., overpotentials) in ECO2R.[78] The free energy change (Fig. 8 (a)) from CO2 molecules to *COOH in the N-rGO structure is 0.97 eV, which is significantly less than that of rGO (2.36 eV), B-rGO(1.92 eV), S-rGO(1.21 eV), and P-rGO (1.35 eV), suggesting that the transfer of electrons from the active sites of N-rGO to the intermediates in N-rGO and the subsequent proton coupling process is more favorable, which ultimately enhances the kinetics of the ECO2R reaction. Combined with the results of electrochemical tests and DFT calculations, N-rGO is a more suitable structure for obtaining good ECO2R catalytic performance, and modification of the electrode based on N-rGO can lead to electrodes with better ECO2R performance. To further probe the impact of di-heteroatom doping into the electrodes on the CO2-to-HCOOH reduction process, DFT calculations on X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB) are carried out. Figure S15 shows the optimized atomic structure models of *COOH adsorbed on the electrode X-rGO (X=NS, NP, NB). In Fig. 8(b), the free energy change for the reaction of CO2 molecules to intermediates in the NS-rGO structure is 0.69 eV, which is remarkably smaller than that of NP-rGO (0.86 eV), and NB-rGO (1.01 eV). The results show that N, S co-doping in di-heteroatom doping can make the electrode require lower Gibbs free energy changes, thereby obtaining better ECO2R electrocatalytic performance. This is probably attributed to the fact that the S atom has a larger atomic radius than the C atom, but the electronegativity is similar, resulting in a high spin density of the surrounding C atoms, which in turn leads to electron spin density redistribution in the electrode.[79,80] Compared with N-rGO, NS-rGO and NP-rGO have lower Gibbs free energy variations, indicating that N, S and N, P co-doping are favorable for enhancing the ECO2R electrocatalytic properties of the electrode, while N, B co-doping has a little effect on the catalytic properties of the electrode. The results of XPS and DFT calculations indicate that the C atom near pyridine N is the main active site in NS-rGO, and S species are used as an auxiliary to adjust the electronic structure of the electrode and reduce the Gibbs free energy formed by the reaction intermediate, thereby improving the ECO2R performance of the NS-rGO electrode. The modeled and free energy variations of different active sites on different types of defects formed in NS-CQDs-rGO-1100 electrodes in ECO2R to formic acid production are shown in Figure S16. In turn, the free energy changes of the best active sites for different types of defects are compared (Fig. 8(c)). The free energy changes of different defect types are all smaller than those of the reactions carried out on the NS-rGO electrode, indicating that the defects have the most significant effect on the ECO2R performance of the electrodes during ECO2R, and that the defects have an extremely important position in the electrodes to carry out the ECO2R process.

In this paper, Vinegar residue is first prepared as graphene with a high degree of graphitization and several layers of structure. Furthermore, a more complete series of heteroatom-doped vertical graphene electrodes (X-rGO, X=N/S/B/P, NS/NP/NB, NSP/NSB/NPB, NSPB) are successfully prepared from vinegar residue-based graphene for electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction (ECO2R). The electrode undergoes the ECO2R process which produces a product of high economic value-formate. The NS-rGO electrodes is proved to have good activity and selectivity for CO2ER to formate (Faraday efficiency (FEHCOO −) = 78.7 %) compared to other different types of catalysts doped with heteroatoms. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are further added to the electrodes to improve the electrode performance, and the defect-rich electrode (NS-CQDs-rGO-1100) is formed by removing heteroatoms from the NS-CQDs-rGO electrode at 1100 °C obtained an electrochemical double layer capacitance of 12.5 mF cm−2 and FEHCOO − up to 90 %, surpassing most of the recently reported state-of-the-art electrocatalysts. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and density functional theory calculations clearly elucidate the intrinsic reasons for the better ECO2R performance of N S co-doped electrode, additional S doping in the electrodes can modulate the electronic structure of the electrode and reduce the Gibbs free energy of the reaction intermediate formation, thus further enhancing the ECO2R performance of the NS-rGO electrode. In addition, the topological defects generated by heteroatom removal are more favorable to improve the ECO2R performance of the electrode than heteroatom doping in the ECO2R process. This work provides a reliable foundation and theoretical basis for the preparation of other vinegar residue-based carbon nanomaterials, and contributes to facilitate the industrialization of ECO2R to formate.
